hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (604 mg/100g) exceeds the 600 mg/100g threshold; high saturated fat (19.53 g/100g) contributes to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Colby cheese to very small portions (15–20 g as a condiment) or choose lower-sodium alternatives such as fresh mozzarella or ricotta, and avoid combining with other high-sodium foods to keep total daily sodium within the recommended 1500–2000 mg limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrates (2.57 g/100g) and high protein (23.76 g/100g) minimise glycaemic impact, but high saturated fat (19.53 g/100g) may worsen insulin resistance with regular overconsumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Colby cheese is compatible with Type 2 Diabetes management in moderate portions (up to 30 g/day) due to its negligible carbohydrate and high protein content; however, saturated fat intake should be monitored to avoid worsening insulin resistance, and total daily sodium should be tracked given the 604 mg/100g sodium level.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (19.53 g/100g) raises LDL cholesterol; high sodium (604 mg/100g) elevates blood pressure risk; cholesterol content is 95 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should restrict Colby cheese to occasional small portions (≤20 g) due to its high saturated fat and sodium content, both of which are independent risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes; substituting with lower-fat, lower-sodium dairy options is advisable for regular consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (19.53 g/100g) may promote pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; exceptional calcium (685 mg/100g) supports bone health in patients on corticosteroids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Colby cheese provides clinically significant calcium (685 mg/100g) beneficial for RA patients at risk of corticosteroid-induced osteoporosis, but its high saturated fat content warrants limiting portions to 20–30 g and pairing with anti-inflammatory foods such as leafy greens and olive oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Colby cheese is a semi-hard American cow's milk cheese originating from Colby, Wisconsin, United States, developed in 1885. It is produced using a washed-curd process in which the whey is partially replaced with water during cheesemaking, resulting in a milder, moister, and more elastic texture compared to cheddar. Colby has an open, porous texture, a pale yellow to orange colour (often from annatto colouring), and a mild, creamy, slightly tangy flavour that becomes more pronounced with age. It is commonly consumed as a snack, in sandwiches, on cheese boards, melted over burgers and nachos, or paired with fruit and crackers. Nutritionally, Colby cheese is energy-dense at 394 kcal/100g, with high fat (32.11 g/100g, of which 19.53 g is saturated), high protein (23.76 g/100g), and very low carbohydrates (2.57 g/100g), making it suitable for ketogenic and low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. It is an excellent source of calcium (685 mg/100g) and phosphorus (457 mg/100g), supporting bone health. Its high sodium content (604 mg/100g) makes it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is not applicable due to negligible carbohydrate content. Colby is often sold in blocks, slices, or as part of Colby-Jack, a marbled blend with Monterey Jack cheese.
